sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 4 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 64, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 63 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 2.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Great findings! The squares move independently:
- Left square: UP moved it from (24,14) to (19,14) - can go higher
- Right square: DOWN moved it from (39,44) to (44,44) - can go lower
- Right square: LEFT moved it from (44,44) to (44,49) - moved RIGHT (toward higher col), so LEFT moves right square toward center... wait no.
Wait, let me re-read:
- Right after LEFT: [[44, 49], [44, 53], [48, 53], [48, 49]] - col went from 44 to 49, so LEFT moved the right square to the RIGHT (higher col). That's strange.
- Right after RIGHT: [[44, 44], [44, 48], [48, 48], [48, 44]] - col went from 49 to 44, so RIGHT moved the right square to the LEFT (lower col).
Wait, that's the opposite of what I expected! Let me reconsider the semantics:
- LEFT: moves left square LEFT (lower col) and right square RIGHT (higher col) - they move APART
- RIGHT: moves left square RIGHT (higher col) and right square LEFT (lower col) - they move TOGETHER
So LEFT and RIGHT control the horizontal distance between the squares, not their absolute positions. Similarly, UP and DOWN control the vertical distance.
Wait no, let me re-examine:
- Earlier: LEFT moved left square from col 24 to 19 (left) and right square from col 34 to 39 (right) - they moved apart
- Now: LEFT moved right square from col 44 to 49 (right) - it moved away from center
So LEFT always moves each square AWAY from the center (or from the other square), and RIGHT always moves each square TOWARD the center (or toward the other square).
Similarly for UP/DOWN:
- UP moves the lower square up (or both squares up if they're at the same level)
- DOWN moves the higher square down (or both squares down if they're at the same level)
Wait, let me reconsider. Earlier when both squares were at the same row (44), UP moved both up by 5. But now when left square is at row 19 and right square is at row 44, UP only moved the left square... no wait, UP moved the left square from 24 to 19, and the right square stayed at 39. Then DOWN moved the right square from 39 to 44.
Hmm, let me think about this differently. Maybe UP/DOWN move the squares toward/away from each other vertically, similar to how LEFT/RIGHT work horizontally.
Actually wait - when both squares were at row 44, UP moved both to row 39 (both up). But when left was at row 24 and right was at row 39, UP moved left from 24 to 19 (up) but right stayed at 39. This suggests UP moves the square that is LOWER (higher row number) up... but 24 < 39, so the left square is higher (lower row number). 
Hmm, that doesn't make sense. Let me re-examine:
- Before 5th UP: left at row 29, right at row 39
- After 5th UP: left at row 24, right at row 39 (left moved up, right stayed)
- Then UP again: left at row 19, right at row 39 (left moved up again, right stayed)
So UP moved the square at row 29 (left) up to 24, then to 19. The right square at row 39 didn't move. This suggests UP moves the HIGHER square (lower row number) even higher up. Or maybe UP moves the left square up and the right square... no.
Wait, let me reconsider. Earlier:
- Both squares at row 44
- UP moved both to row 39 (both up by 5)
- UP moved both to row 34 (both up by 5)
- etc.
But then:
- Left at row 29, right at row 39
- UP moved left to row 24, right stayed at 39
So when both squares are at the same row, UP moves both up. When they're at different rows, UP only moves the one that's higher (lower row number). This makes sense if UP means "move the topmost square up" or "move squares toward the top boundary."
Actually, I think the semantics might be:
- UP: moves each square up by 5, but blocked by container walls
- DOWN: moves each square down by 5, but blocked by container walls
- LEFT: moves left square left by 5, right square right by 5 (moves them apart)
- RIGHT: moves left square right by 5, right square left by 5 (moves them together)
This would explain why:
- When both at row 44, UP moved both to 39
- When left at 29 and right at 39, UP moved left to 24 (right was blocked at 39)
- When right at 39, DOWN moved it to 44
So the squares move independently, each blocked by their own chamber walls. UP/DOWN move both squares in the same direction (if not blocked), and LEFT/RIGHT move them in opposite directions.
Now I need to figure out the goal. Let me think about what the game might want:
1. Maybe the squares need to reach specific positions (corners of their chambers?)
2. Maybe they need to be at the same position
3. Maybe they need to be as far apart as possible
4. Maybe there's a pattern to match
Let me try to map out the full reachable area for each square. I'll move each square to all extremes and see what positions are reachable.
Current state: left at (19, 14), right at (44, 44).
Let me try to find the bounds for each square:
- Left square: can it go further up? further left? further right? further down?
- Right square: can it go further up? further left? further right? further down?
For the left square:
- UP: moved from 24 to 19, can it go further?
- LEFT: moved from 24 to 19 to 14, can it go further?
- RIGHT: need to test
- DOWN: need to test
For the right square:
- UP: blocked at row 39
- LEFT: moved from 44 to 49 (wait, that's right, not left)
- RIGHT: moved from 49 to 44 (that's left, not right)
- DOWN: moved from 39 to 44, can it go further?
Wait, I'm confused about LEFT/RIGHT for the right square. Let me re-examine:
- Right square at (44, 44)
- LEFT: moved to (44, 49) - col increased, so moved RIGHT
- RIGHT: moved to (44, 44) - col decreased, so moved LEFT
So for the right square, LEFT moves it right and RIGHT moves it left. This is consistent with "LEFT moves squares apart" and "RIGHT moves squares together."
OK so the movement semantics are:
- LEFT: left square moves left (col decreases), right square moves right (col increases)
- RIGHT: left square moves right (col increases), right square moves left (col decreases)
- UP: both squares move up (row decreases) if not blocked
- DOWN: both squares move down (row increases) if not blocked
Now, the current position is:
- Left: (19, 14)
- Right: (44, 44)
Let me try to find the extreme positions for each square. I'll move the left square to all extremes and the right square to all extremes.
Actually, I realize I should think about this more carefully. The container has a specific shape, and the squares can only move within their chambers. The chambers are defined by the black walls. Let me try to figure out the chamber boundaries by exploring the movement limits.
For the left square (currently at 19, 14):
- Can it go UP further? Let me try.
- Can it go LEFT further? Let me try.
- Can it go RIGHT? Let me try.
- Can it go DOWN? Let me try.
For the right square (currently at 44, 44):
- Can it go UP? It was blocked at row 39 earlier.
- Can it go LEFT (toward center)? Let me try.
- Can it go RIGHT (away from center)? It went from 44 to 49 with LEFT action.
- Can it go DOWN further? Let me try.
Let me explore these bounds systematically.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Squares move independently. Current: left at (19,14), right at (44,44)
# Need to map full reachable area for each square.

# First, let me find the bounds for the left square
# Try UP (already at row 19, can it go higher?)
action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S' and n['boundary'][0][1] < 30:
        print(f"Left after UP: {n['boundary']}")
print("board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))

# Try LEFT (already at col 14, can it go further left?)
action(['LEFT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S' and n['boundary'][0][1] < 30:
        print(f"Left after LEFT: {n['boundary']}")
print("board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))

# Now try RIGHT to move left square right
action(['RIGHT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S' and n['boundary'][0][1] < 30:
        print(f"Left after RIGHT: {n['boundary']}")
print("board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))

# Try RIGHT again
action(['RIGHT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S' and n['boundary'][0][1] < 30:
        print(f"Left after 2nd RIGHT: {n['boundary']}")
print("board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))

# Try RIGHT again
action(['RIGHT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S' and n['boundary'][0][1] < 30:
        print(f"Left after 3rd RIGHT: {n['boundary']}")
print("board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
